title: Traveling with Kyle - Australia Part 2 - Alice Springs, Uluru, biking through the outback

transcript.text: All right, so yeah, I'm hanging out with these Australian guys for three days, having beer with them.
And there's this one guy, one Australian guy, and he was his birthday, and he wanted to buy me beer.
And he was a cop who was, like, from Tasmania, and he was, like, in between jobs or something.
And he wanted to hang out with me, and he was buying me beer, and then he wanted me to stay with him at his house.
And I went back there. It turned out this guy was gay, and he tried to grab my member.
I was like, whoa, hold on, dude. I'm not gay. I'm not gay, man.
So he finally left me alone.
But he had this little roommate. He was, like, 70 years old and, like, really frail.
And I guess he was a horse jockey that had an accident or something, but he was really funny.
He was, like, totally drunk.
And Australians are funny.
There's lots of those Australians who are just, like, totally, totally just wild, just out of control.
But Darwin is an interesting place because it's smack dab in the middle of Australia, smack dab in the middle of desert.
I don't know how they get their water.
They do have a river that goes through the middle that I guess is a raging river, like, for, like, one week out of the year.
And they'd actually have these big boat races on it.
And I could imagine it was a really big river because it had a really big riverbed.
But in the time, the three days I was there, I was, at one point, this one older, older Aborigine guy came along.
And I guess was kind of an acquaintance or something.
And he was hanging out with me, and he was, like, bragging to me how he was, like, the chief of the aboriginal territory.
And all I had to do was, like, because I had to get special permission in order to pass through the outback between Ayers Rock and Perth.
So, and he said, oh, yeah, just tell him you know me.
And I told him you could go there.
And then you're, and, you know, that's all you need.
And so I went over to the, to the place where you get the permission.
And I said to the lady, oh, yeah, well, so-and-so said that all I had to do was ask his permission.
And she's like, oh, that guy?
No.
And then that gay cop told me that this guy was actually in jail for, like, three years because he killed a white girl and ate her boobs.
A 13-year-old girl.
And he just got out of jail or something.
But, anyways, yeah, so I hung out with these guys for three days.
And finally, one of the guys goes, all right.
And I even hung out with him in one of their hotel rooms because I guess one of them was just visiting and following him around.
And finally, at the end of the day, one of the guys goes, all right, you ready to go get that weed?
I'm like, yeah.
So I got in his old 1970 American supercar, muscle car, and drove out to his house where he was living with his girlfriend.
And I got $200 Australian dollars worth of bud, which, and it was kind bud.
And it was like an ounce, a whole ounce of it, man.
Really good deal.
Like that big, that much.
I was thinking, oh, man, I am sad now.
So, yeah, I got on my bike and I rode down south, rode out southern.
But, yeah, Perth, I mean, Alice Springs is an interesting place because it's just like this oasis.
Like, you know, it's neighborhoods just like America.
People have front yards, backyards, houses.
But then you leave and then you realize you're just in the desert.
But, yeah, I remember pulling up next to a tree and smoking and thinking, this is so cool.
And on that pipe that I found on the side of the road, which is perfect because it had a little cap that I could close it so that I could conserve it.
And, you know, the smoke wouldn't escape and I wouldn't waste anything.
I was thinking, oh, man, this is awesome.
I've got thousands of miles in front of me, tons of bud, you know.
I've got a Walkman with all these cassettes, you know, all these extra batteries.
Let's go.
So I rode, rode down, and then I turned right and went towards Ayers Rock, Uluru.
And I stayed at one of the kind of roadside places one night.
And the guy said, oh, I know you.
These guys told me you were coming.
I guess he was friends with one of the guys I was hanging out and drinking beer with.
And so I hung out with them, and, like, one of the guys, young guys who works there, gave me a tour of his, in his Jeep, of his terrain.
They had, like, thousands of acres, and he took me out, way out to where they feed.
They have this water vat where all of their cows would just migrate from all over and drink it.
And then, I guess, they'd go and brand them and do whatever they needed.
And they were bragging about their meat, like it's the best meat in the world.
And then I rode my bike into Ayers Rock, and that was kind of another crazy oasis-type place.
It was really, they had this tourist place, the menu, like a museum, and then all these campgrounds that I paid to camp out.
And hung out with some of these tourists who I'd met from before over there.
And then, in the morning, did the Ayers Rock, which was beautiful.
It was this huge stone in the middle of the desert that's, like, 400 feet high or something like that.
And I went down to the eastern side of it, and you can actually hike to the top of it from on the eastern side of it.
You can walk up.
And there's only one place in the whole rock, I think, where you can do it, because it, you know, it would come down, just fall vertically down to the ground.
But I went to the top of it, and I was walking around.
I should have walked all over the whole thing, but I just kind of explored the southern part of it.
And then I rode my bike all the way around it.
And, yeah, you can look in every direction.
It's totally flat.
Maybe you see, like, a couple of humps off in the distance, but, yeah, Australia is totally flat.
And then, maybe five miles to the east, there are these other rocks called the Olga rocks, I think,
That are kind of like little mini Ayers rocks, like all next to each other.
And, uh, so the next day I got up and I rode towards the Olgas, and I looked at them.
I didn't, like, ride all through them.
And then I looked at the road, and it just went perfectly west.
So I was heading across the desert from Ayers Rock to towards Perth on this perfectly straight dirt road that was really wide, you know, really hard, but totally corrugated.
But I was on a mountain bike with mountain bike tires, and I had, I had, like, six two-liter water bottles, two of them strapped on my front forks,
Two of them on my handlebars, and then I had, like, a big water bottle, another two-liter water bottle right here in my front water bottle holder right here,
And then two of them in my bags.
So I had one, two, three, four, five, six, seven of them.
Seven two-liter water bottles.
And, uh, so yeah, I just rode all day.
And, um, and at the end of the day I just, uh, sleep on the side of the road.
I think I pitched my tent.
No, no, no, I wasn't pitched my tent later.
Sleep on the side of the road, and so every three hours a car would drive by.
So I pretty much have the whole place to myself, but every few hours I get to, like, a sand pit where I would ride my bike for a little bit on the sand,
But then it would get totally sand pit, and I wouldn't be able to ride my bike at all because the tires would completely sink in the sand, and I had to push my bike.
But I never had to push my bike for more than, like, 100 meters at a time, and then I could ride again.
If it, if it got, like, to the point where I had to push my bike for miles, it would have been bad because it took me, like, exactly two days to get from places.
It seemed to be, like, it was, like, 200 miles between places where I could get water.
It was, uh, and I was able to ride about 100 miles a day, so, and I used up pretty much all my water.
I'd ride all day long, drink half the water, you know, I, I, my, the food was just hamburgers that I would buy at a store,
And then I'd ride 100 miles and get to the next town.
And these little towns, and I stopped at, like, five or six of them on my way across.
These towns were a little, like, aboriginal towns that, um, it's, like, I don't know how many aboriginals, maybe 50 or something,
And living in these different houses in the middle of nowhere, but I always knew where the town was because it would be next to a hill.
Like, it'd be totally flat, and I'd see a hill off in the distance, and I thought, okay, that's where the town is, and then, sure enough, it was right there.
And, uh, so there's one white family that lives in each town.
I stopped at one, and I saw an aboriginal lady, and she's waving to me with, like, six of her dogs.
And I wish I had stayed a whole day there, just touring the place and talking to people, but I didn't.
I passed through.
But I had to, uh, ask the aboriginals who didn't know English where the person who runs the store is, because the store wasn't open.
The one store was, like, as big as a 7-Eleven convenience store.
It wasn't open.
I had to find, because it's only open, like, one day a week or something.
I had to find where the person lived, and the abo would, like, point.
So I went, and I said, okay, yeah, hey, I need to buy something.
And he said, okay, yeah, let's follow me.
So he'd drive his car to the store, and there was, so then, like, one aborigini guy noticed that he was opening it, so he came in.
But he didn't speak any English, and I guess he didn't know, like, how to count money or anything,
Because this guy just picked up a whole pile of stuff off the wall and piled it on the counter.
And the guy who worked there says, okay, Charlie, okay, this costs this much.
And he just threw money down.
He said, okay, you have 15 bucks.
Okay, you're five bucks short.
You're going to have to put some back.
What do you want to put back?
This, this?
This, and he's like, oh, uh-huh.
And he helped him decide what to get, and then he sold me my fixings, my, I guess I bought, I wasn't cooking food.
I just, I guess bought, like, bread and peanut butter and just stuff that I could eat.
And that guy let me sleep in his backyard.
And then I'd ride again in another two days and to the next town.
And, oh, I guess that's the next video.
